verbatim: Hence the young are not fit to be students of Political Science. For they have no experience of life and conduct, and it is these that supply the premises and subject matter of this branch of philosophy. And moreover they are led by their feelings; so that they will study the subject to no purpose or advantage, since the end of this science is not knowledge but action. And it makes no difference whether they are young in years or immature in character: the defect is not a question of time, it is because their life and its various aims are guided by feeling; for to such persons their knowledge is of no use, any more than it is to persons of defective self-restraint.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly states that the young are unfit for political science due to lack of experience and emotional maturity, disqualifying them from the educational resources of this field.

verbatim: Nevertheless it is manifest that happiness also requires external goods in addition, as we said; for it is impossible, or at least not easy, to play a noble part unless furnished with the necessary equipment. [...] also there are certain external advantages, the lack of which sullies supreme felicity, such as good birth, satisfactory children, and personal beauty: a man of very ugly appearance or low birth, or childless and alone in the world, is not our idea of a happy man...

verbatim: For many noble actions require instruments for their performance, in the shape of friends or wealth or political power; [...] As we said therefore, happiness does seem to require the addition of external prosperity, and this is why some people identify it with good fortune (though some identify it with virtue).

verbatim: [§1037] The perfect form of friendship is that between the good, and those who resemble each other in virtue. [...] Hence the friendship of these lasts as long as they continue to be good; and virtue is a permanent quality.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author distinguishes a specific group ('the good' or those with virtue) as the only ones capable of a stable, non-accidental social resource (permanent friendship), contrasting them with 'inferior' or 'bad' men.

verbatim: [§1041] But clearly only good men can be friends for what they are in themselves; since bad men do not take pleasure in each other, save as they get some advantage from each other. [...] whereas the other forms of friendship are liable to be dissolved by calumny and suspicion.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies 'bad men' (those lacking virtue/moral education) as being incapable of a specific immaterial resource: friendship based on the person's inherent self, limiting them to transactional relationships.

verbatim: [§1041] Also friendship between good men alone is proof against calumny; for a man is slow to believe anybody’s word about a friend whom he has himself tried and tested for many years, and with them there is the mutual confidence.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Membership in the group of 'good men' provides a social protection (resource) against slander and suspicion that other groups do not possess.

verbatim: [§1036] With the young on the other hand the motive of friendship appears to be pleasure, since the young guide their lives by emotion [...] hence they both form friendships and drop them quickly, since their affections alter with what gives them pleasure.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The young are defined as a group whose age-based reliance on emotion deprives them of the resource of stable or lasting social attachments.

verbatim: [§1054] Also those blessed with great prosperity have no need of useful friends, but do need pleasant ones, since they desire some society; and though they may put up with what is unpleasant for a short time, no one would stand it continually: you could not endure even the Absolute Good itself for ever, if it bored you; and therefore the rich seek for friends who will be pleasant.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a general social pattern where wealth changes the type of social resources individuals seek, specifically moving away from utility-based relationships toward those providing pleasure and society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to material assistance from friends
verbatim: [1048] Nothing is more characteristic of friends than that they seek each other’s society: poor men desire their friends’ assistance, and even the most prosperous wish for their companionship

verbatim: [§1054] Hence the young make friends quickly, but the old do not, since they do not make friends with people if they do not enjoy their company; and the same applies to persons of a morose temper.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle defines a social capacity based on age groups, where youth is a criterion for easier and faster formation of friendship bonds compared to those of advanced age.

verbatim: [§1054] Morose and elderly people rarely make friends, as they are inclined to be surly, and do not take much pleasure in society; good temper and sociability appear to be the chief constituents or causes of friendship.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly links the criterion of age to a reduced access to the resource of social friendship and societal participation.

verbatim: [§1054] The good man, as we have said, is both useful and pleasant, but the good man does not become the friend of a superior, unless his superior in rank be also his superior in virtue; otherwise the good man as the inferior party cannot make matters proportionally equal.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§1058] Also those blessed with great prosperity have no need of useful friends, but do need pleasant ones, since they desire some society; and though they may put up with what is unpleasant for a short time, no one would stand it continually [...] and therefore the rich seek for friends who will be pleasant.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§1059] But princes and rulers appear to keep their friends in separate compartments: they have some that are useful, and some that are pleasant, but rarely any that are both at once. For they do not seek for friends who are pleasant because they are good, or are useful for noble purposes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text observes that rulers and nobility typically maintain friendships based on utility or pleasure rather than virtue, implying they lack the resource of holistic 'good' friendship due to their social standing and specific needs.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§1062] This is most manifest in the case of the gods, whose superiority in every good attribute is pre-eminent; but it is also seen with princes: in their case also men much below them in station do not expect to be their friends, nor do persons of no particular merit expect to be the friends of men of distinguished excellence or wisdom.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The rule describes how high societal rank (princes) creates a gap that bars those of lower status from entering into friendship with them, asserting that friendship requires a certain level of equality which is absent in these hierarchy-based relations.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§1062] But there is a different kind of friendship, which involves superiority of one party over the other, for example, the friendship between father and son, and generally between an older person and a younger [...] the better of the two parties, for instance, or the more useful or otherwise superior as the case may be, should receive more affection than he bestows.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In age-based relationships, Aristotle identifies a rule where the 'superior' (older) party is entitled to receive more affection than they give, meaning the 'inferior' (younger) group has less access to receiving equal affection.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§1062] The affection rendered in these various unequal friendships should also be proportionate: the better of the two parties, for instance, or the more useful or otherwise superior as the case may be, should receive more affection than he bestows [...] as in the case between husband and wife.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§1071] Most people like receiving honor from men of high station, because they hope for something from them: they think that if they want something, the great man will be able to give it them; so they enjoy being honored by him as a token of benefits to come.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to parental care for welfare

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be governed for own benefit

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§1084] The relation of husband to wife seems to be in the nature of an aristocracy: the husband rules in virtue of fitness, and in matters that belong to a man’s sphere; matters suited to a woman he hands over to his wife.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§1084] And sometimes when the wife is an heiress it is she who rules. In these cases then authority goes not by virtue but by wealth and power, as in an oligarchy.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§1084] The relation between brothers constitutes a sort of timocracy; they are equals, save in so far as they differ in age; hence, if the divergence in age be great, the friendship between them cannot be of the fraternal type.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§1084] Timocracy passes into Democracy, there being an affinity between them, inasmuch as the ideal of Timocracy also is government by the mass of the citizens, and within the property qualification all are equal.

verbatim: [§1115] Such a connection when on stated terms is one of the legal type, whether it be a purely business matter of exchange on the spot, or a more liberal accommodation for future repayment, though still with an agreement as to the quid pro quo; and in the latter case the obligation is clear and cannot cause dispute, though there is an element of friendliness in the delay allowed, for which reason in some states there is no action at law in these cases, it being held that the party to a contract involving credit must abide by the consequences.

verbatim: [§1121] Hence it would appear that a son never ought to disown his father, although a father may disown his son; for a debtor ought to pay what he owes, but nothing that a son can do comes up to the benefits he has received, so that a son is always in his father’s debt. verbatim: [§1223] But the friendship of the good is good, and grows with their intercourse. extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from state surveillance of private life
verbatim: [§1224] Accordingly we shall need laws to regulate the discipline of adults as well, and in fact the whole life of the people generally; for the many are more amenable to compulsion and punishment than to reason and to moral ideals.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author argues that law must extend beyond childhood into adulthood, meaning citizens are subject to compulsory legal discipline throughout their lives.

verbatim: [§1224] Paternal exhortations and family habits have authority in the household, just as legal enactments and national customs have authority in the state... he can count on their natural affection and obedience at the outset.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes the household as a space where the head of the family (father) exercises authority over children based on kinship and benefits.

verbatim: [§1224] The liberal man will need wealth in order to do liberal actions, and so indeed will the just man in order to discharge his obligations... and the brave man will need strength if he is to perform any action displaying his virtue.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle points out that the performance of certain virtues (specifically liberality and justice) requires external material resources, thereby effectively excluding those without wealth from these specific activities.

verbatim: [§1224] ...and every man lives as he likes, in Cyclops fashion laying down the law For children and for spouse.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In states where public law is absent, the text notes the rule that the male head of household dictates the law for his wife and children.

verbatim: [§1225] For pleasure is thought to be especially congenial to mankind; and this is why pleasure and pain are employed in the education of the young, as means whereby to steer their course.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a standard pedagogical practice in his society where children (the young) are specifically managed and directed through the application of pleasure and pain to form their character.

verbatim: [§1229] Hence it appears that true theories are the most valuable for conduct as well as for science; harmonizing with the facts, they carry conviction, and so encourage those who understand them to guide their lives by them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific resource (true theories for conduct) that is accessible to a specific group defined by their capacity to understand them (the educated/intelligent), allowing them to guide their lives differently than the 'mass of mankind'.

verbatim: [§1248] And (α) no one would choose to retain the mind of a child throughout his life, even though he continued to enjoy the pleasures of childhood with undiminished zest
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies children as a distinct group whose mental state (the mind of a child) is inherently less desirable than that of an adult, implying a societal preference for adult cognitive development over the preservation of childhood mental characteristics.

verbatim: [§1244] for granted they are pleasant to ill-conditioned people, it cannot therefore be assumed that they are actually pleasant, except to them, any more than things healthy or sweet or bitter to invalids are really so, or any more than things that seem white to people with a disease of the eyes are really white.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that the perceptions and pleasures of those with health issues (invalids, those with eye disease) are not considered objectively valid or 'actual' by the standards of the society, depriving them of the resource of sensory credibility.

verbatim: [§1246] and it is impossible to feel the pleasures of a just man without being just, or the pleasures of a musician without being musical, and so on.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author argues that specific high-order pleasures are only accessible to those who have achieved a certain level of education or skill (being musical), thereby linking the resource of specific aesthetic enjoyment to the criteria of education/skill.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to the bloom of health
verbatim: [§1258] But the pleasure perfects the activity, not as the fixed disposition does, by being already present in the agent, but as a supervening perfection, like the bloom of health in the young and vigorous.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage identifies the young and vigorous as the specific group that naturally possesses the 'bloom of health', using it as a standard comparison for a supervening perfection.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to exercise intellect on philosophy
verbatim: [§1260] Life is a form of activity, and each man exercises his activity upon those objects and with those faculties which he likes the most: for example, the musician exercises his sense of hearing upon musical tunes, the student his intellect upon problems of philosophy, and so on.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies specific occupational or social groups (musicians, students) and the specific immaterial resources (intellectual pursuit, sensory exercise) they have access to and exercise according to their role.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to exercise hearing on music
verbatim: [§1260] Life is a form of activity, and each man exercises his activity upon those objects and with those faculties which he likes the most: for example, the musician exercises his sense of hearing upon musical tunes, the student his intellect upon problems of philosophy, and so on.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle defines the standard activity (resource) associated with the specific group of musicians in his contemporary society.

verbatim: [§1271] This also holds good of things sweet to the taste: the same things do not taste sweet to a man in a fever as to one in good health; nor does the same temperature feel warm to an invalid and to a person of robust constitution.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle distinguishes between the perceptions of the healthy and the sick, noting that the invalid's sensory experiences are considered deviations from the true or standard quality of the object.

verbatim: [§1272] And if this rule is sound, as it is generally held to be, and if the standard of everything is goodness, or the good man, qua good, then the things that seem to him to be pleasures are pleasures, and the things he enjoys are pleasant. [§1273] It is therefore clear that we must pronounce the admittedly disgraceful pleasures not to be pleasures at all, except to the depraved.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle establishes a social/moral boundary where only the 'good man' (the ethically educated) serves as the standard for what constitutes a legitimate human resource (true pleasure). The 'depraved' are explicitly excluded from this standard and their preferences are dismissed as non-pleasures.

extracted_rules.group: The perfect and supremely happy man
verbatim: [§1273] it is therefore that pleasure, or those pleasures, by which the activity, or the activities, of the perfect and supremely happy man are perfected, that must be pronounced human in the fullest sense. The other pleasures are so only in a secondary or some lower degree, like the activities to which they belong.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author defines a hierarchy of human resources based on the level of 'perfection' or moral/intellectual education. The 'perfected' man has primary access to 'human' pleasure in the fullest sense, while others have access only in a 'secondary or lower degree'.

verbatim: [§1282] (v) Also anybody can enjoy the pleasures of the body, a slave no less than the noblest of mankind; but no one allows a slave any measure of happiness, any more than a life of his own.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly states that while slaves can experience bodily pleasure, society does not grant them the capacity or right to achieve 'happiness' (eudaimonia) or to have a 'life of their own,' framing this as a general rule of status.

verbatim: [§1283] ...the pursuits of politics and war seem to be unleisured—those of war indeed entirely so... But the activity of the politician also is unleisured, and aims at securing something beyond the mere participation in politics—positions of authority and honor...
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author distinguishes between those in active political or military life and those with access to contemplative leisure, noting that the nature of these occupations is inherently 'unleisured' and directed at external ends rather than the activity itself.

extracted_rules.rule: Prince and potentate bodily pleasure pursuit
verbatim: [§1278] But (i) perhaps princes and potentates are not good evidence. Virtue and intelligence, which are the sources of man’s higher activities, do not depend on the possession of power; and if these persons, having no taste for pure and liberal pleasure, have recourse to the pleasures of the body, we must not on that account suppose that bodily pleasures are the more desirable.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text characterizes the wealthy ruling class as often lacking the 'taste' for higher, liberal pleasures, resulting in a focus on bodily amusements rather than intellectual activities.

verbatim: [§1289] But the activity of the politician also is unleisured, and aims at securing something beyond the mere participation in politics—positions of authority and honor, or, if the happiness of the politician himself and of his fellow-citizens, this happiness conceived as something distinct from political activity.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle distinguishes the life of the politician from the life of contemplation by noting that the politician's work is 'unleisured,' meaning it is a form of necessary business rather than an end in itself.

verbatim: [§1293] But the student, so far as the pursuit of his activity is concerned, needs no external apparatus: on the contrary, worldly goods may almost be said to be a hindrance to contemplation; though it is true that, being a man and living in the society of others, he chooses to engage in virtuous action, and so will need external goods to carry on his life as a human being.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that the student/philosopher, unlike the liberal or just man, specifically does not require 'external apparatus' or wealth to perform his primary activity (contemplation).

verbatim: [§1293] Yet if supreme blessedness is not possible without external goods, it must not be supposed that happiness will demand many or great possessions; for self-sufficiency does not depend on excessive abundance, nor does moral conduct, and it is possible to perform noble deeds even without being ruler of land and sea: one can do virtuous acts with quite moderate resources.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues against the idea that only the wealthy (rulers of land and sea) can be virtuous, affirming that those with moderate resources have the resource of participating in noble deeds.

verbatim: [§1297] The liberal man will need wealth in order to do liberal actions, and so indeed will the just man in order to discharge his obligations (since mere intentions are invisible, and even the unjust pretend to wish to act justly).
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies wealth as a necessary resource for the specific social display of 'liberality' and the fulfillment of certain intermediate justice obligations.

verbatim: [§1303] and it is possible to perform noble deeds even without being ruler of land and sea: one can do virtuous acts with quite moderate resources. This may be clearly observed in experience: private citizens do not seem to be less but more given to doing virtuous actions than princes and potentates.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle asserts that the ability to engage in virtuous action is not exclusive to the extremely wealthy or powerful (princes), but is fully accessible to 'private citizens' with 'moderate resources,' effectively defining the resource of noble action as being available to those of middle-wealth groups.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to bodily health and food
verbatim: [§1302] But the philosopher being a man will also need external well—being, since man’s nature is not self—sufficient for the activity of contemplation, but he must also have bodily health and a supply of food and other requirements.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that even for those pursuing the highest intellectual life (philosophers), being human necessitates access to material resources like food and health as a prerequisite for their specific activity (contemplation).

verbatim: [§1304] Solon also doubtless gave a good description of happiness, when he said that in his opinion those men were happy who, being moderately equipped with external goods, had performed noble exploits and had lived temperately; for it is possible for a man of but moderate possessions to do what is right.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle cites Solon to validate the rule that the resource of 'happiness' and 'doing what is right' is granted to those who possess at least moderate property, contrasting them with the necessity of extreme wealth.

verbatim: [§1306] And it seems likely that the man who pursues intellectual activity, and who cultivates his intellect and keeps that in the best condition, is also the man most beloved of the gods. ... it will be reasonable to suppose that they take pleasure in that part of man which is best ... and that they recompense with their favors those men who esteem and honor this most ... Now it is clear that all these attributes belong most of all to the wise man.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author describes a societal/philosophical norm where those who focus on education and the intellect (the wise) are the recipients of divine favor and 'recompense,' a specific immaterial resource.

verbatim: [§1307] And it is difficult to obtain a right education in virtue from youth up without being brought up under right laws; for to live temperately and hardily is not pleasant to most men, especially when young; hence the nurture and exercises of the young should be regulated by law, since temperance and hardiness will not be painful when they have become habitual.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a rule where the state (citing Sparta as the prime example) uses law to compel young people into specific physical and moral exercises, removing their freedom to avoid 'hardy' living.

verbatim: [§1307 / §1310] But as it is, we see that although theories have power to stimulate and encourage generous youths, and, given an inborn nobility of character and a genuine love of what is noble, can make them susceptible to the influence of virtue, yet they are powerless to stimulate the mass of mankind to moral nobility.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author establishes a group boundary based on 'inborn nobility' (lineage/nature), granting this group the immaterial resource of capacity for moral reform through discourse, which is denied to the 'mass of mankind'.

verbatim: [§1307] But doubtless it is not enough for people to receive the right nurture and discipline in youth; they must also practice the lessons they have learnt, and confirm them by habit, when they are grown up. Accordingly we shall need laws to regulate the discipline of adults as well, and in fact the whole life of the people generally.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text argues that legal compulsion is not restricted to the young but extends to adults, meaning grown citizens are subject to state regulation of their daily habits and choices.

verbatim: [§1307] Paternal exhortations and family habits have authority in the household, just as legal enactments and national customs have authority in the state... Moreover individual treatment is better than a common system, in education as in medicine.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In societies where the community neglects regulation, the rule of the head of household (paternal authority) governs the members (children/friends), providing a specific immaterial resource: education tailored to the individual rather than a generic public system.

verbatim: [§1307] But Sparta appears to be the only or almost the only state in which the lawgiver has paid attention to the nurture and exercises of the citizens; in most states such matters have been entirely neglected, and every man lives as he likes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Specifically for the Spartan context mentioned, being a citizen grants access to a state-organized system of physical and moral training (a resource of development), which is absent for citizens in other states.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to public education and discipline
verbatim: [§1315] ...the nurture and exercises of the young should be regulated by law, since temperance and hardiness will not be painful when they have become habitual.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues that the young cannot naturally find temperance pleasant and therefore require legal regulation of their upbringing to instill habituation.

verbatim: [§1316] Accordingly we shall need laws to regulate the discipline of adults as well, and in fact the whole life of the people generally; for the many are more amenable to compulsion and punishment than to reason and to moral ideals.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text posits that even after moving past youth, citizens require laws to regulate their discipline because the majority respond better to compulsion than reason.

extracted_rules.resource: Public regulation of nurture and exercise
verbatim: [§1320] But Sparta appears to be the only or almost the only state in which the lawgiver has paid attention to the nurture and exercises of the citizens; in most states such matters have been entirely neglected, and every man lives as he likes...
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies Sparta as a specific case where the state (the lawgiver) provides the resource of a public system of discipline for its citizens, contrasted with other states.

verbatim: [§1321] Paternal exhortations and family habits have authority in the household, just as legal enactments and national customs have authority in the state, and the more so on account of the ties of relationship and of benefits conferred that unite the head of the household to its other members...
extracted_rules.reasoning: The head of the household (defined by lineage/paternal role) is granted a specific form of authority over other members, parallel to the state's authority over citizens.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to frame laws and discipline
verbatim: [§1324] So presumably a man who wishes to make other people better (whether few or many) by discipline, must endeavor to acquire the science of legislation... only (if at all) by the man with scientific knowledge, just as is the case in medicine and the other professions...
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author argues that the capacity and right to effectively regulate the behavior of others through law should be reserved for those who have acquired 'scientific knowledge' of legislation.
